deductions.reason: The tutor repeatedly walks through all 7 categories in full explanatory lists without ever explicitly articulating why this guided-discovery approach (rather than just telling) supports the student's learning. The tutoring rationale—why the tutor asks questions instead of giving answers—is never stated across any of the three transcripts.

In Transcript 1 Turn 9, the student produces a correct location row entry after struggling. The tutor affirms it briefly but immediately moves on to the next row without prompting reflection on what changed or why the student's approach worked. Similarly in Transcript 2 Turn 9, the student correctly identifies DKI Jakarta as the unit after confusion, and the tutor moves straight to testing it without pausing to reinforce what the student did right methodologically.

In Transcript 1, the tutor cycles through explaining all 7 categories multiple times across turns 1, 2, 3, 6, and 7 without meaningful synthesis or progression. The student repeatedly says they are still confused and the tutor re-explains the same list in slightly different framings rather than reframing, simplifying, or redirecting. In Transcript 3, the same pattern occurs across turns 1-6 where the student keeps asking for broader explanation and the tutor keeps re-listing the same categories without breaking the cycle.

Across multiple turns in Transcripts 1 and 3, the tutor delivers multi-paragraph explanatory lists when shorter, more targeted prompts would have been more appropriate. For example, Transcript 1 Turn 2 gives a full 4-bucket breakdown with sub-bullets, and Transcript 3 Turn 6 lists all 7 categories with what/why for each—both cases where a single focused question would have been more pedagogically effective and appropriately bite-sized.

grade.overview: The tutor maintains a consistently Socratic approach across all three transcripts, never giving submission-ready answers and always redirecting students to do their own reasoning. The tutor avoids rewriting student work and keeps key idea generation student-led. However, there are notable weaknesses: the tutor never explains the rationale for the guided-discovery approach to the student, misses opportunities to prompt reflection after student successes, and repeatedly cycles through the same category-list explanations without synthesizing or reframing when students remain confused. The response length is also frequently excessive, with multi-paragraph lists appearing where focused single questions would serve better.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor consistently uses Socratic questioning, never provides submission-ready content, and keeps reasoning student-led across all three transcripts. 1.2 receives full marks: the tutor builds on student progress (e.g., affirming correct answers before advancing) and generally diagnoses confusion before responding. 1.3 loses 4 points: the tutoring rationale is never explained to students (1.3.A.a), and after student breakthroughs (e.g., T1 Turn 9, T2 Turn 9), the tutor moves on without prompting reflection on what changed (1.3.C.a). 2.1 loses all 4 points: across Transcripts 1 and 3 especially, the tutor repeats the same category-list explanations across 5-6 turns without synthesis, reframing, or suggesting a different approach when the student remains stuck. 3.1 loses all 4 points: the tutor frequently delivers multi-paragraph lists when shorter, more interactive prompts were clearly needed, particularly in Transcripts 1 and 3.
